comment_content:
Covered the complete process of training, finetuning, inference of large language models. Involve considerations of almost everything you should know to develop / improve a LLM (theoretically):
gradient descent,
gpu architecture and scaling,
multi-gpu training and inference,
fast inference techniques,
prompt engineering and finetuning,
RAG and agent,
LLM evaluation

Rather than dive deep into certain topics, this course gives students an overview of a large area. Taking such a course is much more beneficial than searching on internet or reading paper by myself.

This course covered a lot of recent advancements in LLM training and inference and included many research papers published within one year. A paper list is provided after each lecture, but watching lecture videos is much more efficient to fully understand core idea of a paper than reading myself.
comment_teaching: Never went to lectures but watched all recordings. Despite terrible background noise in the videos, the lectures and ppts still helped me a lot in understanding relevant topics.
comment_workload:
Almost 0

It may be a little hard to understand the content at the very beginning, (for me, especially the scaling law), but after some effort one can

The homework is more about environment setup instead of problem solving. Almost no coding is required. The TA provided all codes and you only need to set up your computer environments to run them.

For the homework, you need do the following by yourself (probably by asking AI) if you haven't done before:
run basic machine learning scripts,
setting up vm and generate ssh keys,
apply for TACC platform access and run inference on the docker.
comment_grading:
Homework 20%
Midterm 30%
Final 50%

Exam is not difficult and sample paper is provided. The most important part is the multi-gpu training and inference.

comment_content:
All the lecture sides and homework content can be found here: https://github.com/Relaxed-System-Lab/HKUST-COMP4901Y-2025spring
- ML Preliminary

- Language Model Architecture
- Large Scale Pretrain Overview

- Sequence, MoE parallelism
- Generative Inference
- Inference Algorithm, System Optimization
- Prompt Engineering

Do not trust the prerequisites. Please do take COMP 3211, COMP 3511 and (maybe COMP 3711?) before taking this course.

If you have little experience in AI and if you don't care about grading, I genuinely think this is a pretty cool course you can conside, though there are more calculations and theory than coding.
comment_teaching:
YUAN Binhang is pretty decent at teaching, yet I have trouble following them as they have quite a strong accent. (or maybe I just didn't have the right background as I trusted the prereq)

The slides are quite detailed. If you have background, you can possibly learn from the slides only.

I've only been to one TA session. In that session, the TA does not know how to use projectors. But I don't think they're mandatory anyways.
comment_workload:
Workload is not heavy.

There are in total 4 assignments only. Each assignments only have around 3 questions. I think you can finish the homework with little time if you do understand the course information.
comment_grading:
It's not easy to get good grades from this course. The midterm and the final exam are basically copies of the homework, along with easy MC questions, so you need to expect high mean from this course.

Grading Policy:
4 Homeworks - 20% total
Midterm - 30%
Final - 50%

Stats:
Midterm - 79/100
M: 85 LQ: 79.5
Final - 71/100
M: 79.5 LQ: 73.75

This yields a C+.

comment_teaching: Professor Yuan makes a genuine effort to ensure that students understand the material. He is always willing to explain concepts thoroughly, both during and after class. Since some of the topics can be challenging to grasp at first, you may need to go beyond the slides and take time to digest the content on your own or with the help of AI.
comment_workload: To be honest, this course is quite light in terms of workload. Each homework usually takes less than an hour to complete. Since there are no past papers or sample papers, you don’t have much extra material to go through. Just focus on the lecture slides and prepare a good cheatsheet before the exams — that’s usually enough.
comment_grading:
The grading for this course is quite generous. Professor Yuan aims to give around 25% of students an A-range grade, and he does make that happen.

The homework assignments are relatively lenient. Professor Yuan encourages the TA to award full marks as long as there are no obvious mistakes. Even if your answer differs from the reference solution, it may still be considered correct if it’s reasonable.

Both the midterm and final exams are not particularly difficult. Most students can score nearly full marks on the multiple-choice sections. To do well in this course, it’s important to develop a solid understanding of the different types of parallelism, which are central to the content.

Personally, I got full marks on all homework assignments and scored 93 on both the midterm and final, which earned me an A+ in the course.

The content is more like a computer engineering course instead of a computer science course. If you want to learn about what is the thought behind how to improve Machine Learning under Large-Scale, then DO NOT choose this course. However, if you DO want to know how to implement (or we can say, do the coding staff) for some unknown reason, you may choose this course. (Though I still do not suggest based on Prof. Yuan's poor teaching level.)

Update before final:
A funny thing: Last semester, I took COMP4901Z, which taught RL for one semester. But in this semester, Prof. YUAN tries to use his brilliant teaching skills to teach almost everything in COMP4901Z within one lecture. I think this situation can happen only because I am not smart enough and need to learn RL using one semester.
comment_teaching:
This is the most emotional damage part I want to mention. To be honest, Prof. Yuan's accent is very good and easy to understand. But, his slides are VERY DETAILED so that they make students know what the important part is and what the junior part is. Moreover, I cannot understand his READING style, which may be because I am not a genius and cannot understand the reason that he read his slides again during the whole lecture.
As far as I know, all of my friends learned the content of this course using ChatGPT and outside websites like zhihu or csdn. If you are working or planning to work in this area, I sincerely suggest you learn those staff using the syllabus I provide and outside websites.
By the way, he is a good person and willing to answer questions.
comment_workload: The workload is quite light. There are only four homework. Each homework assignment only needs around 2 hours IF you UNDERSTAND the content he taught in the lecture.
comment_grading:
Not released yet.
He mentioned he would try his best to give everyone a good grade and the only constraint is department requirements. As far as I know, once some instructor says this, she/he will give 20% A, 30% B, and C&D for the rest, which is fair but not good.  I may change this part if the final grade is not the case.

------Updated after final------

Change from B->C

I have to say that no matter how his grading policy is, as long as he does not allow a 50 %+ A range, the grading would be bad. Both mt and final have a narrow distribution:

mt: LQ-79.5 Median-85 UQ-91
final: LQ-74 Median-79 UQ-87

which means two multiple-choice questions will guide you from UQ to mean or from mean to LQ. I do not think it is fair for anyone.

This course briefly introduces various topics but is not really in-depth. And there exists very little/no hands-on chance in various topics
One thing to note is that the first half of this course is more related to optimization/COMP2611 rather than AI/ML, so if you are not familiar with/ dislike computer organization/ optimization (e.g. you are not COMP/CPEG and just +AI student), You may suffer in this course.
